medical.title: NHS patients are being socially prescribed yoga—but is yoga ready to help them?
medical.summary: Yoga can be seen as an accessible way to exercise and improve well-being. You don't always need a gym membership, specialist machinery or other people to do it.

medical.title: Lived experience is often dismissed—but we should recognize it as a form of expertise
medical.summary: Institutions increasingly invite people to contribute their lived experience. Government agencies appoint patients to advisory panels and call on communities for their views on policy. Health New Zealand employs peer support workers, and universities seek lived experience in research. These initiatives acknowledge that lived experience matters.

medical.title: AI turns mouse movements into language-like tokens, uncovering autism-related social behavior patterns
medical.summary: An artificial intelligence model capable of reading and interpreting animal behavior like language has been developed by researchers at KAIST. The team created an AI model that learns behavioral data in a manner similar to natural language and was able to independently identify social behavioral deficits in an autism mouse model, opening a new avenue for interpretable neuroscience.

medical.title: AI can be a personal trainer in your pocket—but is it safe?
medical.summary: Generative artificial intelligence (AI) is changing the fitness industry: People can now ask chatbots to write marathon plans, build gym programs and even adjust workouts based on sleep or heart rate data.

medical.title: Infant iron supplements linked to fewer behavior problems at age three
medical.summary: Healthy, breastfed infants who receive iron supplementation show fewer aggressive behaviors at age 3 compared with children who did not receive supplementation, according to new research from Umeå University. However, more research is needed before iron supplementation can be recommended for all infants. The paper is published in Scientific Reports.

medical.title: One gene, two diseases: Study reveals opposing dementia and cancer risks
medical.summary: Researchers at the University of Kentucky Sanders-Brown Center on Aging have uncovered evidence that a single genetic variant may influence the risk of two of the diseases people fear most—dementia and cancer—but in opposite ways.

medical.title: Global reforms fall short as psychosis care still brings abuse, coercion and exclusion
medical.summary: People with psychosis continue to face abuse, discrimination and early death despite global reform efforts to protect their human rights, according to La Trobe University researchers.

medical.title: Stopping skin cancer before it starts could cut its health care costs
medical.summary: A new study has revealed the hundreds of thousands of skin cancer appointments and medical procedures Queenslanders are attending and undergoing each year, prompting experts to call for further investment in prevention campaigns to protect public health and ease pressure on the health care system.

medical.title: Innovative soft robotic heart offers new way to study disease and test life-saving devices
medical.summary: Researchers at UNSW Sydney have developed a fully synthetic soft robotic heart that reproduces the complex movements and internal structures of the human heart, opening the door to better treatments, safer medical devices and more personalized care.

medical.title: Advancing personalized medicine requires close collaboration across stakeholders
medical.summary: The development of personalized medicine depends on collaboration among multiple stakeholders, who must navigate uncertainty while shaping expectations of future value. A recent study from the University of Eastern Finland published in Innovation examines how companies take part in this process and what kinds of challenges and opportunities emerge.

medical.title: Faulty calcium signaling may drive dry mouth in Down syndrome, raising gum disease risk
medical.summary: Researchers at NYU College of Dentistry have uncovered what may be biologically driving oral health issues unique to Down syndrome. Their study, published in Cell Reports, describes a molecular mechanism—a defect in calcium signaling—behind low saliva production, along with other factors that may contribute to gum disease.

medical.title: Rare muscle disorder mutations reveal a precision medicine strategy
medical.summary: Scientists at the University of California San Diego have uncovered how genetic mutations cause a rare group of inherited neuromuscular disorders and identified promising new strategies to correct them, including a potential new use for an existing antidepressant.

medical.title: Next-generation HIF-2α inhibitor shows promise in kidney cancer translational clinical trial
medical.summary: The first-in-patient, single-arm ARC-20 clinical trial, led by Dana-Farber Cancer Institute's Toni K. Choueiri, MD, has evaluated casdatifan, a next-generation HIF-2α inhibitor, as monotherapy for metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC), the most common subtype of kidney cancer. Patients eligible for the study were previously treated with standard therapies.

medical.title: TROP2 marks relapse-driving colorectal cancer cells and opens path to targeted treatment
medical.summary: A team led by researchers from the German Cancer Research Center (DKFZ) and the HI-STEM Stem Cell Institute has discovered a promising new approach to treating advanced colorectal cancer. The study, published in Nature, identifies a key marker of particularly aggressive and treatment-resistant colorectal cancer cells.

medical.title: Long COVID rehab program helps with return to work and focus
medical.summary: Ten weeks of cognitive rehabilitation can help people with long COVID symptoms such as brain fog achieve their goals in returning to work and hobbies, a new clinical trial led by University College London (UCL) researchers has found.

medical.title: Neurobiologists improve symptoms of Huntington's disease with targeted brain stimulation in mice
medical.summary: Huntington's disease is a devastating brain disorder in which decaying nerve cells lead to progressively worsening cognitive and movement abilities. While the genetic mutation responsible for the condition is well known, the intricate details of how the disease disrupts specific brain circuits have not been clearly understood. This gap has complicated efforts to develop effective therapies, and the disease remains fatal, with no known cure.

medical.title: Long-lived families reveal a rare genetic clue to healthy aging
medical.summary: A study of long-lived families has identified rare genetic variants that may help people stay healthier for much longer as they age. One standout mutation appears to temper inflammation, potentially delaying disease and extending years of healthy living.

science.title: World's first synthetic cell with a complete life cycle could revolutionize biological engineering
science.summary: While many of life's mysteries remain unsolved, every biologist can describe the basic processes performed by a living organism, including energy use, reproduction, growth and development. While these characteristics can be replicated in isolation in a lab, the idea of a completely synthetic biological organism has long been relegated to science fiction.

science.title: New bioelectronic microdevices enable remote cell stimulation using ultrasound
science.summary: The Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona (UAB) and the Institute of Microelectronics of Barcelona (IMB-CNM-CSIC) have developed a new generation of wireless piezoelectric microdevices capable of electrically stimulating living cells at an individual level. The study, recently published in the journal Small and chosen as the cover image, demonstrates how these microdevices can convert mechanical forces, whether produced by the cells themselves or applied externally via ultrasound, into electrical signals that enable the controlled and noninvasive activation of cellular processes.

science.title: The universe is less uniform than we thought—cosmology may need a radical rethink
science.summary: Modern cosmology rests on a simple assumption: If we look on large enough scales, matter should be distributed evenly, with no preferred direction within the cosmos. This is known as the cosmological principle.

science.title: Mobile learning output expanded rapidly from 2017 to 2026, analysis of 2,500 papers shows
science.summary: A bibliometric analysis of mobile learning research published between 2017 and 2026 shows a sharp expansion in output. There was a big surge between 2020 and 2022 associated with pandemic-driven shifts in higher education. Mobile learning (m-learning), defined as the use of mobile devices such as smartphones and tablets to support educational activity, has grown alongside global device penetration and intensive daily usage among students, according to the study published in the International Journal of Mobile Learning and Organisation.

science.title: Climate resilience of brown bears over 175,000 years revealed in 3D analyses of their jaws
science.summary: European brown bears are masters of survival: These animals have weathered Pleistocene climate fluctuations and survived the cycles of ice ages and interglacial periods to the present day. Zoologists have now investigated the origins of this evolutionary flexibility: 3D analyses of bear jaws show that certain chewing structures in the lower jaw of brown bears have apparently adapted very flexibly to changing climatic conditions. SNSB zoologist Anneke van Heteren has now published her findings in the journal Comptes Rendus Palevol.

science.title: 13,000 tons of space junk clutters Earth orbit. Here's how it could be cleaned up
science.summary: Seventy years ago, Earth had only one satellite: the moon. Now it has more than 15,000—about 10,000 of which are owned by Elon Musk's SpaceX. The world's first trillionaire plans to launch 1 million more satellites, each roughly 70 meters (230 feet) long and 20 meters (66 feet) wide, that would form a data center megaconstellation.

science.title: Prickly starfish and urchins are decimating Australia's reefs. But we could find ways to protect them
science.summary: Australia is home to some of the world's most beautiful reefs. This includes the lush Great Southern Reef, which wraps around Australia's southern coastline, and the world-renowned Great Barrier Reef.

science.title: NASA Seeks Volunteers for New Yearlong Simulated Moon, Mars Mission
science.summary: NASA is recruiting research participants for the agency’s next simulated deep space mission. Beginning no earlier than August 2027, research volunteers will spend one year living and working in interplanetary environments at the agency’s Johnson Space Center in Houston, operating under isolated conditions expected during crewed missions to the Moon or Red Planet. Insights from this […]

science.title: Tooth fossil analysis suggests 'brawn before bite' in early Asian mammals
science.summary: An analysis of fossil teeth from mammals that lived in China following the most recent major mass extinction suggests size came before both shape and function as diets diversified.

science.title: 'I hate you!': What little kids really mean when they say this
science.summary: "I hate you. You're the worst mum in the world!" It's a sentence that can feel heartbreaking to parents. You try to set a boundary with your little one, and they lash out with "I hate you."

science.title: Martian dust storms may generate atmospheric electrical conditions that could impact future missions
science.summary: A new study by a doctoral researcher at The University of Alabama in Huntsville (UAH), part of The University of Alabama System, suggests global dust storms on Mars may organize the Martian atmosphere into regions favorable for electrical activity, increasing the potential for electrostatic discharges that could affect missions to the red planet by interfering with electronics, causing arcing between conductive surfaces, and damaging exposed scientific instruments and spacecraft systems. The research, conducted by Chali Idosa Uga, a third-year Ph.D. student in the Department of Space Science at UAH, is published in The Planetary Science Journal.

science.title: LINK Spacecraft Set for Mission to Boost NASA’s Swift Observatory
science.summary: A first-of-its-kind mission to raise the orbit of NASA’s Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory is poised for launch no earlier than Thursday, July 2, 5:09 a.m. EDT (9:09 p.m. UTC+12), from Kwajalein Atoll, part of the Republic of the Marshall Islands in the South Pacific Ocean. A robotic servicing spacecraft called LINK, built by Katalyst Space, will blast […]

science.title: ESA outlines high-tech lander instruments for 2050 Enceladus
science.summary: Saturn's moon Enceladus has become a prime solar system target for astrobiologists. This is because the small moon, which is just over 10% the diameter of Earth's moon, harbors a vast subsurface ocean beneath its icy crust. This subsurface ocean, combined with the geysers at Enceladus' south pole that discharge bits of this ocean into the void, provides scientists with a treasure trove of opportunities for scientific research into whether Enceladus could harbor ingredients for life as we know it, or even direct evidence of life.

science.title: Orbit overload could devastate astronomy if 1.7 million proposed satellites brighten night sky
science.summary: A new European Southern Observatory (ESO) study has found that current proposals to launch more than 1.7 million satellites into orbit, including extremely bright ones, would have "devastating consequences for astronomy." According to the study, no more than 100,000 faint satellites, below naked-eye visibility, should orbit Earth to safeguard our ability to observe the night sky with modern telescopes.

science.title: Urokodia! 518-million-year-old fossil shows beginning of spider's bite
science.summary: The earliest evidence of spiders' fangs has been identified in a 518-million-year-old fossil by scientists at the University of Leicester and Yunnan University.

science.title: NASA’s Webb Studies How Planet Survived Death of its Star
science.summary: NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope is giving us new insight into the far-future of solar systems like our own, as the agency continues to reveal the secrets of the universe and our place in it. Billions of years ago, a Sun-like star nearing the end of its life swelled tremendously in size to become a […]

science.title: Curiosity Blog, Sols 4934-4940: In the Land of the Polygons
science.summary: Written by William Farrand, Senior Research Scientist, Space Science Institute Earth planning date: Friday, June 26, 2026 There were two planning cycles over this span of sols. The Monday planning took place with Curiosity situated within a unit that from orbital imagery appeared light-toned, and from earlier rover positions appeared smooth. Reaching this unit, the […]

science.title: From fields to space farming, new tool detects crop drought stress before it's visible
science.summary: When it comes to drought stress, timing can be the difference between saving a crop and losing it, whether in a greenhouse or in the high-stakes environment of future space missions. In a recent study published in Plant Phenomics, researchers with the University of Florida Institute of Food and Agricultural Sciences (UF/IFAS), the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) and NASA used hyperspectral imaging to detect stress in lettuce plants shortly after watering was reduced.

science.title: Perfluorooctanoic acid in the Seto Inland Sea: Variability, transport, and fate
science.summary: Perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) is one of the per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), a class of substances within a broader universe of organofluorine compounds. PFOA has potential adverse effects on human health and environmental safety because of its toxicity and bioaccumulation. Its innate chemical stability, widespread use, and long-range transport result in PFOA's ubiquity in the global environment.

science.title: Video: Thousands of planets are hidden in this image
science.summary: Millions of stars. Thousands of hidden worlds. One unprecedented view of our galaxy. Three years since launch, ESA's Euclid space telescope reveals the center of the Milky Way galaxy in extraordinary detail: a mosaic of tens of millions of stars captured in just 26 hours. But this is more than an image. It is a map of stellar evolution, from dark clouds where stars are being born to ancient populations packed into the galactic bulge.

science.title: Woodcock charge deer to defend nests, footage reveals
science.summary: American woodcock, short, plump shorebirds with long, thin beaks, are widely known for their bobbing stride and nasally "peent" calls, but not for being aggressive. Yet one April afternoon, when a deer sniffed around a woodcock hen's ground nest looking for food, the hen lunged at it, scaring it away.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

mix_items.title: Franţa acordă încă un concediu plătit după nașterea sau adopția unui copil. Ambii părinți pot beneficia de el
mix_items.summary: Începând de miercuri, părinţii din Franţa vor avea dreptul la un nou concediu de naştere, o măsură de susţinere a natalităţii aflate în declin de mai mulţi ani.

mix_items.summary: În perioada 8–12 iulie 2026, Asociația Elite Art organizează la Sibiu cea de-a XVI-a ediție a festivalului Classics for Pleasure, sub tema Perpetuum Vibratio. Ediția din acest an explorează ideea… Articolul CLASSICS FOR PLEASURE 2026: PERPETUUM VIBRATIO 8–12 iulie | Sibiu apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: Edenred marchează 10 ani de voluntariat prin Ideal Day, inițiativa care susține creșterea oamenilor, comunităților și o societate fără risipă alimentară. mix_items.summary: Fostul fotbalist, actual drojdier, Gabriel Tamaş acuză o mare nedreptate în noua lege a stadioanelor! Potrivit lui Tamaş, deşi e un lucru bun că berea va fi în sfârşit permisă la meciuri, e „absolut revoltător” că de aceasta se vor putea bucura doar suporterii din galerie, nu şi jucătorii de pe teren. „După ce că […] The post Tamaș, revoltat de legea care permite berea pe stadion: „Pot să bea doar suporterii, nu și jucătorii!” appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: 😂 Hămesiților! După apa din Vidraru, francezii ne-au furat și apa de la metrou
mix_items.summary: Nu-i mai satură Dumnezeu! Înțelegem că este caniculă și în țara lor, dar asta nu le dă dreptul francezilor să ne fure apa chiar în halul ăsta! După ce ne-au golit lacul Vidraru și au dus în Franța toată apa, i-a venit rândul apei de la metrou să fie mutată din subteranele bucureștene în Hexagon. […] The post Hămesiților! După apa din Vidraru, francezii ne-au furat și apa de la metrou appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.summary: Liceul Politehnica București, Opera Comică pentru Copii și Programul Național Cantus Mundi lansează primul model de educație liceală dedicat industriei spectacolelor. Pentru prima dată în România, elevii vor putea urma,… Articolul Primul liceu din România unde se vor pregăti artiști de musical și specialiști în sunet și lumini pentru spectacole apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: Veşti bune pentru călătorii care aveau nevoie să ajungă de la Gara de Nord la Piaţa Victoriei! Deşi autorităţile nu au reuşit să pompeze apa de pe linia metroului, a fost identificată o soluţie de avarie. Potrivit reprezentanţilor ISU, Metrorex şi ai Căpităniei Bucureşti-Ilfov, vaporaşul din Herăstrău va fi adus cât de repede posibil în […] The post Soluţie de avarie! mix_items.summary: Ediția a VIII-a a Festivalului SoNoRo Musikland va avea loc în perioada 17-27 iulie 2026 și se va desfășura în Sibiu, Avrig, Porumbacu de Sus, Copșa Mare, Sighișoara, Meșendorf, Criț, Viscri și Poiana Brașov.… Articolul Ediția a VIII-a a Festivalului SoNoRo Musikland va avea loc în perioada 17-27 iulie 2026 și se va desfășura în Sibiu, Avrig, Porumbacu de Sus, Copșa Mare, Sighișoara, Meșendorf, Criț, Viscri și Poiana Brașov. apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: După o creștere de 38% a volumelor de roșii românești achiziționate în 2025 față de anul anterior, Lidl estimează pentru sezonul 2026 o nouă creștere, de aproximativ 30%, ceea ce… Articolul 100% roșii românești la raft: Lidl România marchează un an record al achizițiilor de la producătorii locali apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: Centrul de Proiecte al Municipiului Timișoara organizează o serie de două ateliere dedicate medierii culturale, adresate profesioniștilor din sectorul cultural, studenților și tuturor celor interesați de modul în care arta… Articolul Ateliere de mediere culturală | Practici de conectare între artă, spații și comunități, de la Bienala de la Veneția la Spațiile pentru Comunitate ale Timișoarei apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... mix_items.summary: Zbor Hub Brașov a găzduit finala națională Arena Urșilor Junior 2026, o competiție dedicată liceenilor cu idei de afaceri curajoase și planuri antreprenoriale inovatoare. Brașovul a devenit, pentru câteva ore, … Read More The post Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026 appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

mix_items.title: World's first synthetic cell with a complete life cycle could revolutionize biological engineering
mix_items.summary: While many of life's mysteries remain unsolved, every biologist can describe the basic processes performed by a living organism, including energy use, reproduction, growth and development. While these characteristics can be replicated in isolation in a lab, the idea of a completely synthetic biological organism has long been relegated to science fiction.

mix_items.title: World Vision România investește în formarea viitorilor specialiști ai agriculturii românești. O seră didactică modernizată cu tehnologii de Agricultură 4.0, inaugurată la Liceul Agricol „Sandu Aldea” din Călărași.
mix_items.summary: București, 20 mai 2026: Fundația World Vision România, împreună cu Liceul Agricol „Sandu Aldea” din Călărași, a inaugurat o seră... mix_items.title: Ateliere în comunitate, sprijin pentru dezvoltarea copiilor
mix_items.summary: Atelierele în comunitate organizate de Fundația Inocenți îi ajută pe copii să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile emoționale și relațiile sociale. The post Ateliere în comunitate, sprijin pentru dezvoltarea copiilor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: NHS patients are being socially prescribed yoga—but is yoga ready to help them?
mix_items.summary: Yoga can be seen as an accessible way to exercise and improve well-being. You don't always need a gym membership, specialist machinery or other people to do it.

mix_items.title: Povestea Alexandrei – Un pas spre încredere, un pas spre viitor
mix_items.summary: Alexandra, adolescentă sprijinită de Fundația Inocenți, și-a recăpătat încrederea în sine și motivația pentru învățare prin suport educațional și emoțional. The post Povestea Alexandrei – Un pas spre încredere, un pas spre viitor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: New bioelectronic microdevices enable remote cell stimulation using ultrasound
mix_items.summary: The Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona (UAB) and the Institute of Microelectronics of Barcelona (IMB-CNM-CSIC) have developed a new generation of wireless piezoelectric microdevices capable of electrically stimulating living cells at an individual level. The study, recently published in the journal Small and chosen as the cover image, demonstrates how these microdevices can convert mechanical forces, whether produced by the cells themselves or applied externally via ultrasound, into electrical signals that enable the controlled and noninvasive activation of cellular processes.

mix_items.summary: Fundația World Vision România solicită Camerei Deputaților respingerea proiectul de lege privind finanțarea ONG-urilor, adoptat de Senat (L233/2026). Inițiativa legislativă... Articolul World Vision România: Propunerea legislativă privind finanțarea ONG-urilor aduce măsuri disproportionate care riscă să afecteze copiii vulnerabili din România apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: The universe is less uniform than we thought—cosmology may need a radical rethink
mix_items.summary: Modern cosmology rests on a simple assumption: If we look on large enough scales, matter should be distributed evenly, with no preferred direction within the cosmos. This is known as the cosmological principle.

mix_items.title: Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi
mix_items.summary: Ionuț, elev în clasa a XI-a la un liceu din Municipiul Calafat, pasionat de muzică, cu un glas folcloric aparte,... Articolul Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Mobile learning output expanded rapidly from 2017 to 2026, analysis of 2,500 papers shows
mix_items.summary: A bibliometric analysis of mobile learning research published between 2017 and 2026 shows a sharp expansion in output. There was a big surge between 2020 and 2022 associated with pandemic-driven shifts in higher education. Mobile learning (m-learning), defined as the use of mobile devices such as smartphones and tablets to support educational activity, has grown alongside global device penetration and intensive daily usage among students, according to the study published in the International Journal of Mobile Learning and Organisation.

mix_items.title: Climate resilience of brown bears over 175,000 years revealed in 3D analyses of their jaws
mix_items.summary: European brown bears are masters of survival: These animals have weathered Pleistocene climate fluctuations and survived the cycles of ice ages and interglacial periods to the present day. Zoologists have now investigated the origins of this evolutionary flexibility: 3D analyses of bear jaws show that certain chewing structures in the lower jaw of brown bears have apparently adapted very flexibly to changing climatic conditions. SNSB zoologist Anneke van Heteren has now published her findings in the journal Comptes Rendus Palevol.

mix_items.title: 13,000 tons of space junk clutters Earth orbit. Here's how it could be cleaned up
mix_items.summary: Seventy years ago, Earth had only one satellite: the moon. Now it has more than 15,000—about 10,000 of which are owned by Elon Musk's SpaceX. The world's first trillionaire plans to launch 1 million more satellites, each roughly 70 meters (230 feet) long and 20 meters (66 feet) wide, that would form a data center megaconstellation.

mix_items.title: Lived experience is often dismissed—but we should recognize it as a form of expertise
mix_items.summary: Institutions increasingly invite people to contribute their lived experience. Government agencies appoint patients to advisory panels and call on communities for their views on policy. Health New Zealand employs peer support workers, and universities seek lived experience in research. These initiatives acknowledge that lived experience matters.

mix_items.title: AI turns mouse movements into language-like tokens, uncovering autism-related social behavior patterns
mix_items.summary: An artificial intelligence model capable of reading and interpreting animal behavior like language has been developed by researchers at KAIST. The team created an AI model that learns behavioral data in a manner similar to natural language and was able to independently identify social behavioral deficits in an autism mouse model, opening a new avenue for interpretable neuroscience.

mix_items.title: Prickly starfish and urchins are decimating Australia's reefs. But we could find ways to protect them
mix_items.summary: Australia is home to some of the world's most beautiful reefs. This includes the lush Great Southern Reef, which wraps around Australia's southern coastline, and the world-renowned Great Barrier Reef.

mix_items.title: AI can be a personal trainer in your pocket—but is it safe?
mix_items.summary: Generative artificial intelligence (AI) is changing the fitness industry: People can now ask chatbots to write marathon plans, build gym programs and even adjust workouts based on sleep or heart rate data.

mix_items.title: Infant iron supplements linked to fewer behavior problems at age three
mix_items.summary: Healthy, breastfed infants who receive iron supplementation show fewer aggressive behaviors at age 3 compared with children who did not receive supplementation, according to new research from Umeå University. However, more research is needed before iron supplementation can be recommended for all infants. The paper is published in Scientific Reports.

mix_items.title: NASA Seeks Volunteers for New Yearlong Simulated Moon, Mars Mission
mix_items.summary: NASA is recruiting research participants for the agency’s next simulated deep space mission. Beginning no earlier than August 2027, research volunteers will spend one year living and working in interplanetary environments at the agency’s Johnson Space Center in Houston, operating under isolated conditions expected during crewed missions to the Moon or Red Planet. Insights from this […]

mix_items.title: One gene, two diseases: Study reveals opposing dementia and cancer risks
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the University of Kentucky Sanders-Brown Center on Aging have uncovered evidence that a single genetic variant may influence the risk of two of the diseases people fear most—dementia and cancer—but in opposite ways.

mix_items.title: Tooth fossil analysis suggests 'brawn before bite' in early Asian mammals
mix_items.summary: An analysis of fossil teeth from mammals that lived in China following the most recent major mass extinction suggests size came before both shape and function as diets diversified.

mix_items.title: 'I hate you!': What little kids really mean when they say this
mix_items.summary: "I hate you. You're the worst mum in the world!" It's a sentence that can feel heartbreaking to parents. You try to set a boundary with your little one, and they lash out with "I hate you."

mix_items.title: Global reforms fall short as psychosis care still brings abuse, coercion and exclusion
mix_items.summary: People with psychosis continue to face abuse, discrimination and early death despite global reform efforts to protect their human rights, according to La Trobe University researchers.

mix_items.title: Martian dust storms may generate atmospheric electrical conditions that could impact future missions
mix_items.summary: A new study by a doctoral researcher at The University of Alabama in Huntsville (UAH), part of The University of Alabama System, suggests global dust storms on Mars may organize the Martian atmosphere into regions favorable for electrical activity, increasing the potential for electrostatic discharges that could affect missions to the red planet by interfering with electronics, causing arcing between conductive surfaces, and damaging exposed scientific instruments and spacecraft systems. The research, conducted by Chali Idosa Uga, a third-year Ph.D. student in the Department of Space Science at UAH, is published in The Planetary Science Journal.

mix_items.title: Stopping skin cancer before it starts could cut its health care costs
mix_items.summary: A new study has revealed the hundreds of thousands of skin cancer appointments and medical procedures Queenslanders are attending and undergoing each year, prompting experts to call for further investment in prevention campaigns to protect public health and ease pressure on the health care system.

mix_items.title: LINK Spacecraft Set for Mission to Boost NASA’s Swift Observatory
mix_items.summary: A first-of-its-kind mission to raise the orbit of NASA’s Neil Gehrels Swift Observatory is poised for launch no earlier than Thursday, July 2, 5:09 a.m. EDT (9:09 p.m. UTC+12), from Kwajalein Atoll, part of the Republic of the Marshall Islands in the South Pacific Ocean. A robotic servicing spacecraft called LINK, built by Katalyst Space, will blast […]

mix_items.title: Innovative soft robotic heart offers new way to study disease and test life-saving devices
mix_items.summary: Researchers at UNSW Sydney have developed a fully synthetic soft robotic heart that reproduces the complex movements and internal structures of the human heart, opening the door to better treatments, safer medical devices and more personalized care.

mix_items.title: Advancing personalized medicine requires close collaboration across stakeholders
mix_items.summary: The development of personalized medicine depends on collaboration among multiple stakeholders, who must navigate uncertainty while shaping expectations of future value. A recent study from the University of Eastern Finland published in Innovation examines how companies take part in this process and what kinds of challenges and opportunities emerge.

mix_items.title: ESA outlines high-tech lander instruments for 2050 Enceladus
mix_items.summary: Saturn's moon Enceladus has become a prime solar system target for astrobiologists. This is because the small moon, which is just over 10% the diameter of Earth's moon, harbors a vast subsurface ocean beneath its icy crust. This subsurface ocean, combined with the geysers at Enceladus' south pole that discharge bits of this ocean into the void, provides scientists with a treasure trove of opportunities for scientific research into whether Enceladus could harbor ingredients for life as we know it, or even direct evidence of life.

mix_items.title: Orbit overload could devastate astronomy if 1.7 million proposed satellites brighten night sky
mix_items.summary: A new European Southern Observatory (ESO) study has found that current proposals to launch more than 1.7 million satellites into orbit, including extremely bright ones, would have "devastating consequences for astronomy." According to the study, no more than 100,000 faint satellites, below naked-eye visibility, should orbit Earth to safeguard our ability to observe the night sky with modern telescopes.

mix_items.title: Faulty calcium signaling may drive dry mouth in Down syndrome, raising gum disease risk
mix_items.summary: Researchers at NYU College of Dentistry have uncovered what may be biologically driving oral health issues unique to Down syndrome. Their study, published in Cell Reports, describes a molecular mechanism—a defect in calcium signaling—behind low saliva production, along with other factors that may contribute to gum disease.

mix_items.title: Rare muscle disorder mutations reveal a precision medicine strategy
mix_items.summary: Scientists at the University of California San Diego have uncovered how genetic mutations cause a rare group of inherited neuromuscular disorders and identified promising new strategies to correct them, including a potential new use for an existing antidepressant.

mix_items.title: Next-generation HIF-2α inhibitor shows promise in kidney cancer translational clinical trial
mix_items.summary: The first-in-patient, single-arm ARC-20 clinical trial, led by Dana-Farber Cancer Institute's Toni K. Choueiri, MD, has evaluated casdatifan, a next-generation HIF-2α inhibitor, as monotherapy for metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC), the most common subtype of kidney cancer. Patients eligible for the study were previously treated with standard therapies.

mix_items.title: TROP2 marks relapse-driving colorectal cancer cells and opens path to targeted treatment
mix_items.summary: A team led by researchers from the German Cancer Research Center (DKFZ) and the HI-STEM Stem Cell Institute has discovered a promising new approach to treating advanced colorectal cancer. The study, published in Nature, identifies a key marker of particularly aggressive and treatment-resistant colorectal cancer cells.

mix_items.title: Urokodia! 518-million-year-old fossil shows beginning of spider's bite
mix_items.summary: The earliest evidence of spiders' fangs has been identified in a 518-million-year-old fossil by scientists at the University of Leicester and Yunnan University.

mix_items.title: Long COVID rehab program helps with return to work and focus
mix_items.summary: Ten weeks of cognitive rehabilitation can help people with long COVID symptoms such as brain fog achieve their goals in returning to work and hobbies, a new clinical trial led by University College London (UCL) researchers has found.

mix_items.title: Neurobiologists improve symptoms of Huntington's disease with targeted brain stimulation in mice
mix_items.summary: Huntington's disease is a devastating brain disorder in which decaying nerve cells lead to progressively worsening cognitive and movement abilities. While the genetic mutation responsible for the condition is well known, the intricate details of how the disease disrupts specific brain circuits have not been clearly understood. This gap has complicated efforts to develop effective therapies, and the disease remains fatal, with no known cure.

mix_items.title: NASA’s Webb Studies How Planet Survived Death of its Star
mix_items.summary: NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope is giving us new insight into the far-future of solar systems like our own, as the agency continues to reveal the secrets of the universe and our place in it. Billions of years ago, a Sun-like star nearing the end of its life swelled tremendously in size to become a […]

mix_items.title: Curiosity Blog, Sols 4934-4940: In the Land of the Polygons
mix_items.summary: Written by William Farrand, Senior Research Scientist, Space Science Institute Earth planning date: Friday, June 26, 2026 There were two planning cycles over this span of sols. The Monday planning took place with Curiosity situated within a unit that from orbital imagery appeared light-toned, and from earlier rover positions appeared smooth. Reaching this unit, the […]

mix_items.title: From fields to space farming, new tool detects crop drought stress before it's visible
mix_items.summary: When it comes to drought stress, timing can be the difference between saving a crop and losing it, whether in a greenhouse or in the high-stakes environment of future space missions. In a recent study published in Plant Phenomics, researchers with the University of Florida Institute of Food and Agricultural Sciences (UF/IFAS), the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) and NASA used hyperspectral imaging to detect stress in lettuce plants shortly after watering was reduced.

mix_items.title: Perfluorooctanoic acid in the Seto Inland Sea: Variability, transport, and fate
mix_items.summary: Perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) is one of the per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), a class of substances within a broader universe of organofluorine compounds. PFOA has potential adverse effects on human health and environmental safety because of its toxicity and bioaccumulation. Its innate chemical stability, widespread use, and long-range transport result in PFOA's ubiquity in the global environment.

mix_items.title: Video: Thousands of planets are hidden in this image
mix_items.summary: Millions of stars. Thousands of hidden worlds. One unprecedented view of our galaxy. Three years since launch, ESA's Euclid space telescope reveals the center of the Milky Way galaxy in extraordinary detail: a mosaic of tens of millions of stars captured in just 26 hours. But this is more than an image. It is a map of stellar evolution, from dark clouds where stars are being born to ancient populations packed into the galactic bulge.

mix_items.title: Woodcock charge deer to defend nests, footage reveals
mix_items.summary: American woodcock, short, plump shorebirds with long, thin beaks, are widely known for their bobbing stride and nasally "peent" calls, but not for being aggressive. Yet one April afternoon, when a deer sniffed around a woodcock hen's ground nest looking for food, the hen lunged at it, scaring it away.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: Long-lived families reveal a rare genetic clue to healthy aging
mix_items.summary: A study of long-lived families has identified rare genetic variants that may help people stay healthier for much longer as they age. One standout mutation appears to temper inflammation, potentially delaying disease and extending years of healthy living.

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Cum merge dieta? - Excelent. O admir de la distanta.

satire_ro.title: Potop şi trăsnete în Bucureşti după ce Anca Alexandrescu l-a trădat pe trimisul lui Dumnezeu
